content.content:
apiVersion: v1
data:
  mongod.conf: |
    storage:
      dbPath: /data/db
      wiredTiger:
        engineConfig:
          journalCompressor: zlib
    net:
      bindIpAll: true
    replication:
      replSetName: mongodb-replica
    security:
      authorization: enabled
      keyFile: /etc/mongo-keyfile/keyfile
    systemLog:
      verbosity: 1
    operationProfiling:
      mode: slowOp
      slowOpThresholdMs: 200

input.command: kubectl get secret admin-user-password -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' 2>&1 | base64 -d

content.thinking:
Now I have the current configuration:
- `systemLog.verbosity: 1` → increase by 1 → set to `2`
- `operationProfiling.slowOpThresholdMs: 200` → 2x → set to `400`
- `storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.journalCompressor: zlib` → change to `snappy`

The approach:
1. Update the ConfigMap with the new settings
2. Rolling restart the StatefulSet pods one at a time to apply the changes, ensuring data remains available

However, WiredTiger journal compression is a storage engine setting that can only be changed when the data directory is empty or not yet initialized. 

Actually, I need to reconsider this—the `journalCompressor` setting affects the write-ahead log compression, not the collection data itself. This means I can change it via a clean shutdown and config update, though existing journal files won't be recompressed. The new journal entries after restart will use the new compressor. So when I change the `journalCompressor` setting and restart MongoDB, existing journal files keep their original compression while new ones use the updated compressor—this is safe to do. My approach is to update the ConfigMap, then perform a rolling restart of the StatefulSet, being careful to restart secondaries first before stepping down and restarting the primary to maintain replica set availability.

With `kubectl rollout restart`, the StatefulSet will restart pods in reverse ordinal order (2, 1, 0), and as long as I wait for each pod to rejoin before moving to the next, I keep the replica set available since I only need 2 out of 3 members up at any time.

For the journalCompressor change specifically, MongoDB documentation confirms that changing compression settings on an existing replica set requires a rolling restart—the new compression applies to journal files when mongod starts, while existing data files remain unaffected. So my approach is straightforward: update the ConfigMap, then perform the rolling restart, ideally starting with the secondary pods and leaving the primary for last.

content.text:
Current config shows:
- `verbosity: 1` → increase to `2`
- `slowOpThresholdMs: 200` → double to `400`
- `journalCompressor: zlib` → change to `snappy`

Let me check who the current primary is, then update the ConfigMap and do a rolling restart.

input.command: kubectl exec -n mongodb mongodb-replica-0 -- mongosh --quiet "mongodb://admin-user:admin123password@localhost:27017/admin" --eval "rs.isMaster().ismaster + ' ' + rs.isMaster().me" 2>&1
kubectl exec -n mongodb mongodb-replica-0 -- mongosh --quiet "mongodb://admin-user:admin123password@localhost:27017/admin" --eval "rs.status().members.forEach(m => print(m.name, m.stateStr))" 2>&1

content.thinking:
Pod-0 is the PRIMARY. The rolling restart will go in reverse order (2, 1, 0), so secondaries first and primary last - perfect.

Now let me update the ConfigMap with the new values:
- verbosity: 1 → 2
- slowOpThresholdMs: 200 → 400
- journalCompressor: zlib → snappy

Then trigger a rolling restart.

content.text: Pod-0 is PRIMARY. Rolling restart goes 2→1→0, so secondaries restart first — ideal. Now update the ConfigMap:

input.command:
kubectl patch configmap mongod-config -n mongodb --patch '{"data":{"mongod.conf":"storage:
  dbPath: /data/db
  wiredTiger:
    engineConfig:
      journalCompressor: snappy
net:
  bindIpAll: true
replication:
  replSetName: mongodb-replica
security:
  authorization: enabled
  keyFile: /etc/mongo-keyfile/keyfile
systemLog:
  verbosity: 2
operationProfiling:
  mode: slowOp
  slowOpThresholdMs: 400
"}}' 2>&1
input.description: Patch mongod-config ConfigMap with updated settings

content.thinking:
Good. The ConfigMap is updated. Now I need to do a rolling restart. I'll restart the pods one at a time, starting with the secondaries (2 and 1) and ending with the primary (0).

The most reliable way is to manually delete/restart pods one at a time and wait for each to become ready before proceeding.

I'll use `kubectl rollout restart` which will handle this automatically with the StatefulSet's rolling update strategy (reverse ordinal order: 2→1→0).

Actually, `kubectl rollout restart` for a StatefulSet will trigger the rolling update policy. The default `updateStrategy.type: RollingUpdate` will roll out pods in reverse ordinal order, waiting for each pod to become Ready before proceeding to the next.
Now I'll trigger the rolling restart and monitor its progress.

content.text: ConfigMap verified. Now trigger the rolling restart — StatefulSet restarts in reverse order (2→1→0), keeping 2/3 members up throughout:

input.description: Trigger rolling restart of the StatefulSet

input.command:
# Poll for rollout completion, checking every 10s, up to 5 minutes
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  STATUS=$(kubectl rollout status statefulset/mongodb-replica -n mongodb --timeout=10s 2>&1)
  echo "[$i] $STATUS"
  if echo "$STATUS" | grep -q "successfully rolled out"; then
    break
  fi
  sleep 10
done
